planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; June displays the best color contrast in partial shade with some morning sun — deep shade causes the golden center to fade to chartreuse.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; June is one of the most celebrated hostas ever introduced — named the Hosta of the Year by the American Hosta Society in 2001 and consistently one of the top-ranked hostas for its spectacular golden-yellow to chartreuse center surrounded by a wide margin of deep blue-green to gray-blue, creating one of the most vivid and striking bicolor hosta foliage patterns available. Each leaf displays the centered gold with the blue-green margin in a broad, heart-shaped form that makes this medium-sized hosta (18–24 inches tall, 30–42 inches wide) one of the most beautiful foliage plants for the shade garden. 'June' is a sport of 'Halcyon' — the reverse of the parent's cool blue coloration — and the two combine beautifully to create complementary foliage effects. In partial shade, the gold center develops its richest, most saturated butter-gold color; in deep shade, it fades toward chartreuse. In zone 9, hostas prefer deep shade and consistent moisture — the intense summer heat stresses hostas more than in their preferred zone 3–7 range, and consistent irrigation with organic mulch is essential for success. June emerges in early spring with particularly attractive spring color and produces lavender flowers in mid-summer. Divide every 4–5 years in early spring.
sources: American Hosta Society — Hosta of the Year 2001

common_name: Frances Williams Hosta (Siebold Hosta, Blue-Green Corrugated Hosta)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade; Frances Williams is a large, slow-growing hosta that takes several years to reach full size — plant 3–4 feet apart and allow it to develop undisturbed for 5–7 years for maximum impact.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade; Frances Williams is one of the most iconic and historically important hostas ever introduced — a sport of Hosta sieboldiana 'Elegans' discovered by Frances Williams of Sharon, Connecticut, in 1936, and still one of the most distinctive and recognizable hostas in cultivation. The immense, deeply corrugated, blue-green leaves (8–14 inches long and almost as wide) are irregularly margined with a wide band of golden-yellow to creamy-yellow that creates a dramatic bicolor effect — the corrugated surface texture, blue-green center, and yellow margin working together to produce a truly architectural, bold foliage plant. At full maturity, Frances Williams forms a substantial specimen 2–2.5 feet tall and 4–6 feet wide — a statement shade plant that commands attention. The deeply corrugated, textured leaf surface is characteristic of the Hosta sieboldiana heritage and provides outstanding slug resistance compared to smooth-leafed hostas. Frances Williams is slow to mature and should not be divided frequently — allow 5–7 years for a division to reach impressive size. Pale lavender to near-white flowers emerge in early summer on scapes just above the foliage. Best in deep to partial shade in zone 9; consistent moisture and organic mulch are essential.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Hameln blooms earliest of the common Pennisetum alopecuroides cultivars — typically beginning in late July in zone 9, which is an advantage over later-blooming forms that may not flower before the first frost in colder zones.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Hameln is the most widely planted dwarf fountain grass cultivar — a compact selection of Pennisetum alopecuroides reaching 24–36 inches in both height and spread, making it the most garden-friendly size class of fountain grass for typical residential landscapes. The soft, arching, medium-green blades produce dense, mounding clumps from which upright flower scapes rise in summer, bearing the characteristic bottle-brush or fox-tail plumes in creamy white to slightly rosy-pink, aging to tan through fall and winter. Hameln is one of the earliest-blooming Pennisetum alopecuroides cultivars, typically beginning in late July in zone 9 — this early bloom is advantageous in zone 9 where the long growing season allows plants to reach maximum plume development. The dried seed heads and foliage provide exceptional winter interest, remaining attractive through February when the plant should be cut back to 4–6 inches. Unlike many fountain grass selections that self-seed aggressively, Hameln is considered relatively sterile or low-seeding — reducing invasive spread concerns. Outstanding for mass planting, border edging, and container culture. Extremely drought-tolerant once established; essentially pest-free.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Awabuki is cold-sensitive below zone 8 — plant in a protected location in zone 8, or choose the more cold-hardy V. odoratissimum (standard sweet viburnum) instead.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Awabuki is the most attractive and most dramatic viburnum for Gulf Coast zone 8–10 landscapes — a vigorous, large-growing evergreen shrub or small tree producing enormous, glossy, dark-green leaves with an extraordinary mirror-like surface sheen that catches and reflects light unlike any other commonly grown landscape shrub. The leaves (4–8 inches long) have such a pronounced, lacquered surface gloss that the plant appears polished — the deep, wet-looking gloss is so intense it almost looks artificial. This exceptional foliage texture makes Awabuki a striking specimen plant and creates a more architectural, tropical effect than standard sweet viburnum. White, fragrant flower clusters (panicles) appear in spring, followed by small red berries that age to black and attract birds. The fast, vigorous growth makes Awabuki excellent for rapid screening and privacy hedges — it can grow 3–4 feet per year in zone 9 with adequate water and fertilizer. Awabuki is more cold-sensitive than standard sweet viburnum (V. odoratissimum) and can suffer significant damage in zone 8 during severe winters; zone 9 coastal gardens are ideal. Prune after spring bloom to control size — Awabuki tolerates heavy pruning and can be maintained as a formal hedge.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Golden oregano's leaf color is brightest (most intense golden-yellow) in full sun — in shade the leaves revert toward chartreuse-green. Do not plant in rich, moist soil — oregano requires dry, lean conditions to develop concentrated flavor.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; Golden oregano is an attractive, low-growing, spreading oregano cultivar with bright golden-yellow foliage that functions simultaneously as a culinary herb and a decorative ground cover — the vivid chartreuse to golden leaf color brightens sunny herb gardens, rock gardens, and container compositions while the leaves retain the characteristic Mediterranean oregano flavor for culinary use. The low, mat-forming habit (6–12 inches tall, spreading indefinitely) makes it useful as a ground cover on dry, sunny slopes and as a fragrant edging plant along paths. In summer, small pink to purple flowers appear in clusters, attracting bees and butterflies. Golden oregano's flavor is milder than standard Greek or Italian oregano — the ornamental leaf color comes at some cost to the pungent oil concentration that gives oregano its intense flavor. For the most flavorful culinary herbs, grow standard green oregano varieties alongside Golden oregano. Like all Mediterranean herbs, Golden oregano is extremely drought-tolerant once established and actually develops the most concentrated flavor in lean, dry soil with limited irrigation. Perfect drainage is essential — waterlogged roots cause rapid crown rot, particularly in hot, humid Gulf Coast summers.
